title: Input and Output
description: Putting cout, cin, data types, and operators together: reading different types, chaining cin reads, and a small preview of output formatting.
objectives: Read values of different types (int, double, char) with cin
Combine cin input with variables and operators to compute a result
Understand how cin handles whitespace between chained reads
Get a first preview of output formatting with setprecision
Build a small complete program using everything learned so far

sections.text: We've covered cout and cin, several data types, and operators separately. This topic is about combining them — reading real input, computing something with it, and printing a result.

cin >> works the same way regardless of type — it reads text typed by the user and converts it to match the variable's type. Typing 25 into an int works, 19.99 into a double works, and A into a char works.

sections.code:
int a, b, c;
cin >> a >> b >> c;

sections.text: Recall from cout & cin that >> can be chained. Whether the user types 1 2 3 on one line, or presses enter between each number, cin >> automatically skips spaces and newlines between values — it only cares about the actual values, not how they're separated.

This ties together several past lessons at once: cin reads two ints, + adds them, static_cast<double> (from Type Casting) forces real division instead of truncating integer division, and cout prints the results. For example, typing 7 and 4 would print Sum: 11 and Average: 5.5.

This is only a preview — setprecision comes from a different header, <iomanip>, and controls how many digits cout displays for decimal numbers. We're not using it yet; it's mentioned so the name isn't a surprise when we reach a dedicated formatting topic later.

sections.title: A calculator's display
sections.text: Think of cin as the calculator's number pad — however you press the buttons, it reads the numbers you intend. cout is the calculator's screen — it shows the result of whatever operation was performed on those numbers. Input and Output are just the two ends of that same conversation.

sections.title: Common Beginner Mistakes
items: Forgetting a variable must exist and have the right type before cin >> can read into it.
Expecting int / int to give a decimal average — cast to double first, as covered in Type Casting.
Assuming cin >> a >> b; requires input on separate lines — it accepts values separated by any whitespace, spaces or newlines.
Trying to read a decimal like 19.99 into an int — it will only capture the whole-number part, since int variables can't hold decimals.

sections.title: Interview Questions

questions.question: Does cin >> care whether input is separated by spaces or newlines?
questions.answer: No — cin >> automatically skips whitespace, including spaces and newlines, between values.

questions.question: How would you read two integers and print their average as a decimal?
questions.answer: Read them with cin >> a >> b;, sum them, then cast the sum (or one operand) to double before dividing by 2, so the division isn't truncated by integer division.

questions.question: What happens if you try to read a decimal value into an int variable with cin?
questions.answer: Only the whole-number portion is captured, since an int cannot store a fractional part.

sections.thirtySecondAnswer: cin >> can read values of any basic type — int, double, char — converting the typed text to match the variable's type, and it automatically skips whitespace between chained reads like cin >> a >> b;. Combining cin, variables, operators, and cout lets us build small complete programs: read values, compute a result using the right casts to avoid integer-division surprises, and print it back with cout. Output formatting details like setprecision come from a separate header, <iomanip>, and are covered later.

sections.title: Trick to Remember Input and Output
sections.text: cin = the calculator's number pad. cout = the calculator's screen. Whitespace between cin >> reads doesn't matter — only the values do.
